title: practical english usage - Part 133
content:
I have little interest in politics. Few politicians are really honest.

we  use fɑ)/eu/with plurals. Compare: We   use the determiner (a) little with singular (usually uncountable) words, and 1  uncountable         and   plural 168  (a) little     and   (a) few For most in superlatives (e.g. the most beautiful), ►    Section  17.

They all talk a lot, but your little girl talks (the) most. The truth hurts most.

informal style.

(The) most can also be used as an adverb.The is often dropped in an 4 the    most      as  an   adverb Who earns (the) most money in your family?

The is sometimes dropped in an informal style.

Susan found the most blackberries.

used with the before nouns.

In comparisons (when most means 'more than any other/others’ ) it is normally 3  the    most      (= 'more    than    any   other/others')        with    nouns Some people had difficulty with the lecture, but most understood.

We   can drop a noun after most if the meaning is clear.

2  most      without      a  noun I've read most of Shakespeare.  The Romans conquered most of England.

However, most of can be used directly before personal and geographical names.

Most of it is imported.       Most of them can sing a little.

Most of our fruit is imported.   Most of these people can sing a little.

- Most fruit is imported.      - Most people can sing a little.

Before another determiner (article, possessive, etc) or a pronoun, we  use not Most children like ice cream, (     The most children-...) we  do not use the before most in this case.

We   can use most as a determiner before uncountable or plural nouns. Note that 1  most      (of) 167 most                              ___ For no more, not any more/longer, ►    535.      For far more, much  more, many more, etc. ►    207.

For more in comparatives (e.g. more comfortable), ►    Section  17.

I hate this job more and more as the years go by.

More and more is used to talk about continual increase.

I couldn't agree more.

More can also be used as an adverb.

4 more      as  an   adverb grammar •  168   (a) little and (a) few For a little with comparatives (e.g. a little better), ►    207. ɪ .       For the adjective little, ►    580.

visits to Scotland. This is rather formal.

Note that few can follow possessives in expressions like his few friends, my few 6 his    few     friends,        etc but not They had little hope, (          Their hope was little.) not after be.

(A) little and (a) few are determiners. They  are normally used before nouns, but 5 not   used    after    be 'Some more soup? 'Just a little, please.' We   can drop a noun and use (a) little/few alone, if the meaning is clear.

4 (a) little     and   (a) few     without      nouns He's got very little patience and very few friends.

However, very little and very few are possible in an informal style.

Only a few people remembered my birthday.

Come on! We haven't got much  time!

or only a little/few.

(e.g. ordinary conversation), we  generally prefer not much/many, Little and few (with no article) are rather formal. In an informal style 3  formal     and   informal       language We've got quite a few friends in the village.

Quite a few (informal) means 'a considerable number'.

His ideas are difficult, but a few people understand them.

- His ideas are difficult, and few people understand them.

Give the roses a little water every day. (not a lot, but some) - Cactuses need little water, (not much water) Compare: You don't need to go shopping. We've got a few potatoes and some steak.

Would you like a little soup?

They  may suggest ideas like 'better than nothing’  or 'more than expected'.

A little and a few are more positive: their meaning is generally closer to some.

Few people can speak a foreign language perfectly.

The average MP  has little real power.

many as expected', and similar ideas.

none. They  may suggest ‘ not as much/many as one would like', ‘ not as much/ Without a, little and few usually have rather negative meanings, close to no or There is a difference between little and a little, and between few and a few.
